Bags I almost bought in Japan 🇯🇵

Vintage luxury shopping in Osaka is dangerous in the best way. The prices were tempting. The selection was unreal. And yes… some of these were real deals.

But I’m learning something important:

A good price is not the same thing as the right purchase.

Building a fine jewelry and luxury collection on purpose means walking away sometimes — even when you can afford it.

I’d rather regret not buying it than regret buying it for the wrong reason.

Which one would you have taken home?

And more importantly… are you a deal hunter or a disciplined collector?

Save this if you’re building your collection intentionally.

This weekend, I went vintage shopping for an Hermes Birkin bag in the most unexpected place: DISNEYLAND! 

On Main Street between the Penny Arcade and the Fortuosity Shop, the New Century Jewelry Shop in Disneyland sells vintage designer handbags from @whatgoesaroundnyc. I’ve spotted Gucci, Chanel and Louis Vuitton before but this was the first time I’ve spotted a Birkin!

On my last visit, they had a 35cm Hermes Birkin from 1997 in pristine condition for $19,500. 

Who knew Disneyland sold Birkin bags! What do you think? Would you buy a luxury handbag at an amusement park?

The Hermès deal that almost got me 😭

I really thought I found the one a Birkin priced way under what I’d been seeing everywhere else in Japan. Too good to be true… and it kind of was.

When you’re luxury shopping secondhand, especially in places like Japan, you have to read every detail carefully. Some bags look perfect at first glance, but the fine print matters more than the price tag.

This one taught me a lot about what to look for, what questions to ask, and why patience is part of the process when you’re hunting for your dream bag.

Still grateful for the experience and honestly, it made finding the right one even sweeter ✨

Addressing the allegation that Hermes makes their bags in China in this last segment of my 3-part Reels video.  This is a flat-out LIE, and that part of the viral video was really meant to mislead people. And sadly so many believed it because let's be honest, it's a good excuse to justify and validate buying fakes without feeling guilty (and for lower prices) 💀 But that piece of information shared was really meant to misinform, and was just a ploy to convince customers to buy their counterfeit Hermes bags ✌🏻 

The reality is, as of my post today, Hermes is the only remaining widely-known luxury maison that 100% manufactures their bags (especially the Birkin, Kelly, Constance) in France. There are other smaller, independent brands that do (Joseph Duclos is an independent luxury brand that also manufacures all their bags 100% in France). Sad that the other mainstream "luxury" brands no longer do this because they stand to make bigger margins if they produce elsewhere (France has very high labor costs for highly-skilled artisans).

The crazy prices these mainstream designer brands command today factor in the money they invested in prime real estate locations of their boutiques, as well as a lot of marketing and communications to continue elevating & maintaining their brand value (hiring top celebrities as "ambassadors" to front campaigns, giving out free bags to influencers/press, social and traditional media ad spend, traveling fashion shows, etc). But they sadly cut costs on materials and labor. There is a lack of transparency in their supply chains despite their efforts to publicly pledge their commitments for "sustainability" and manufacturing products in "greener" ways (it's all just PR) 💀

So the bottomline is this: if you plan on buying a very costly bag from any of these "offending" mainstream/popular designer brands that are categorized as "luxury,", think long and hard before purchasing. These brands really need to do better because we as consumers who pay good money for their products, deserve better.

🔴 Paris Fashion Week : coup d’éclat devant le défilé @hermes ! Des « policières » surgissent en voiture 🚓🚨👮‍♀️ pancartes et slogans à l’appui :
📢 « Hermès : STOP aux peaux ‘exotiques’ » 
📢 « Tuer pour s’habiller : délit de cruauté »
Derrière les célèbres sacs « Birkin » et « Kelly » d’Hermès, qui peuvent coûter plus de 35 000 €, se cache une cruauté insoutenable. La peau des alligators sert à fabriquer des bracelets de montre, tandis que deux à trois crocodiles sont tués pour chaque sac à main.
🐊 Aucun accessoire de mode ne justifie une telle souffrance. Exigeons qu’Hermès renonce aux peaux « exotiques » !

The Hermès deal that almost got me 😭

I really thought I found the one a Birkin priced way under what I’d been seeing everywhere else in Japan. Too good to be true… and it kind of was.

When you’re luxury shopping secondhand, especially in places like Japan, you have to read every detail carefully. Some bags look perfect at first glance, but the fine print matters more than the price tag.

This one taught me a lot about what to look for, what questions to ask, and why patience is part of the process when you’re hunting for your dream bag.

Still grateful for the experience and honestly, it made finding the right one even sweeter ✨

Biri Hermès Birkin’in ilham kaynağı, diğeri ise Lady Dior’un zamansız zarafetini simgeleyen kraliyet figürüydü. 👑

Fransa Dışişleri Bakanlığı’nda gerçekleşen bu davet, Prenses Diana’nın Fransa’ya yaptığı resmî ziyaret kapsamında düzenlendi ve sanat, siyaset ile moda dünyasından seçkin isimleri bir araya getirdi. 👜

Bu akşam, yalnızca bir devlet yemeği değil; Fransız–İngiliz ilişkilerinin, stilin ve kültürel etkinin kesiştiği tarihî bir andı. ✨

Karuppusamy 

We stumbled upon him by the roadside, surrounded by colorful wires and years of quiet dedication. 
We showed him a Prada crochet bag and asked if he could make something like it. 
He glanced at it, smiled, and said, “It has a bit more design, but I can make it… maybe for ₹900–₹1000.”

We told him the original sells for ₹1,83,000. He looked up, wide-eyed — not out of jealousy or disbelief, but with a soft chuckle that said, “The world is strange.”

For over two decades, he and his family have been weaving beauty beneath the shade of a tree.

No brand name, no fancy showroom, just hands that know the rhythm of craft, and hearts full of quiet pride.

This is a story of value, not of price tags, but of patience, passion, and the kind of skill that’s passed down, not bought.

what’s in Jane Birkin’s Birkin?

In Agnès Varda’s dreamy 1988 portrait Jane B. par Agnès V., the legendary Jane Birkin literally empties her Hermes Birkin bag onto the steps of a Paris courtyard — a jumble of notes, makeup, pens and humble everyday objects spilling out as she looks at the camera with a wry smile. Rather than revealing secrets, Birkin playfully suggests that showing what’s in the bag reveals “very little,” turning a simple fashion moment into a meditation on identity, memory and the myths we build around style and celebrity.

The scene has since become a cultural touchstone — a proto “What’s in My Bag?” before that trend ever existed — and a reminder that the woman behind the name is far richer than any accessory could suggest.
Birkin’s life as an actress, singer, muse and icon, documented with intimacy and warmth by Varda, makes this moment less about luxury and more about the lived experience of a free spirit navigating art, aging, and self-definition.

🎥: Jane B. par Agnès V. 
  Directed by Agnès Varda in 1988 - France

Addressing the allegation that Hermes makes their bags in China in this last segment of my 3-part Reels video.  This is a flat-out LIE, and that part of the viral video was really meant to mislead people. And sadly so many believed it because let's be honest, it's a good excuse to justify and validate buying fakes without feeling guilty (and for lower prices) 💀 But that piece of information shared was really meant to misinform, and was just a ploy to convince customers to buy their counterfeit Hermes bags ✌🏻 

The reality is, as of my post today, Hermes is the only remaining widely-known luxury maison that 100% manufactures their bags (especially the Birkin, Kelly, Constance) in France. There are other smaller, independent brands that do (Joseph Duclos is an independent luxury brand that also manufacures all their bags 100% in France). Sad that the other mainstream "luxury" brands no longer do this because they stand to make bigger margins if they produce elsewhere (France has very high labor costs for highly-skilled artisans).

The crazy prices these mainstream designer brands command today factor in the money they invested in prime real estate locations of their boutiques, as well as a lot of marketing and communications to continue elevating & maintaining their brand value (hiring top celebrities as "ambassadors" to front campaigns, giving out free bags to influencers/press, social and traditional media ad spend, traveling fashion shows, etc). But they sadly cut costs on materials and labor. There is a lack of transparency in their supply chains despite their efforts to publicly pledge their commitments for "sustainability" and manufacturing products in "greener" ways (it's all just PR) 💀

So the bottomline is this: if you plan on buying a very costly bag from any of these "offending" mainstream/popular designer brands that are categorized as "luxury,", think long and hard before purchasing. These brands really need to do better because we as consumers who pay good money for their products, deserve better.

Within a year it had become my full time job, and over the years gave me the opportunity to travel the world, meet so many of you, make friends all over the world, and collaborate with my dream brands… sharing the highs and lows of life along the way 🩷 THANK YOU for all of the love and support. Me: slight obsession with #early2000s #diorsaddlebags Also me: seems perfectly reasonable 😂

That #gallianofordior #ss2000 runway show was the stuff of fashion legend and it was the first time we’d ever laid eyes on a Saddle Bag - #carriebradshaw toted one throughout #satcseason3 it was very much her ‘Aidan’ bag and is possibly the most synonymous bag with the show. 
I’ve owned and sold several over the years but I always wanted to nab this one - I guess good things do come to those who wait 😉 it’s only been 25 years….
